section.title: Glorify God in Your Body
section.sectionRationale: Some in Corinth had turned a slogan of Christian freedom, everything is permissible, into a license for sexual sin, treating the body as morally irrelevant. Paul answers with the highest theology of the body in the New Testament: the body is for the Lord, our bodies are members of Christ, and each believer's body is a temple of the Holy Spirit. Because we were bought at a price, we are not our own, and so the body is a place to glorify God, not to indulge the flesh.
section.thesis: Christian freedom is real but never limitless: the body is not made for immorality but for the Lord, joined to Christ and indwelt by the Spirit, so the redeemed, who are not their own but bought at a price, must flee sexual immorality and glorify God in the body.
section.frameNote: Three movements: freedom weighed and the body claimed for the Lord; union with Christ against union with immorality; the body as the Spirit's temple, bought at a price, made to glorify God.

units.label: Free, but not your own master
units.note: the body is for the Lord

verses.text: “Everything is {{exestin|permissible}} for me,” but not everything is beneficial. “Everything is permissible for me,” but I will not be mastered by anything.
“Food for the stomach and the stomach for food,” but God will destroy them both. The body is not intended for sexual immorality, but for the Lord, and the Lord for the body.
By His power God raised the Lord from the dead, and He will raise us also.

units.label: Members of Christ, not of immorality

verses.text: Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Shall I then take the members of Christ and unite them with a prostitute? Never!
Or don’t you know that he who unites himself with a prostitute is one with her in body? For it is said, “The two will become one flesh.”
But he who unites himself with the Lord is one with Him in spirit.
Flee from {{porneia|sexual immorality}}. Every other sin a man can commit is outside his body, but he who sins sexually sins against his own body.

units.label: A temple bought at a price

verses.text: Do you not know that your body is a {{naos|temple}} of the Holy Spirit who is in you, whom you have received from God? You are not your own;
you were {{agorazo|bought}} at a price. Therefore {{doxazo|glorify}} God with your body.

exestin.gloss: It is permitted, it is lawful. Paul quotes a Corinthian slogan celebrating freedom, then qualifies it twice: not everything is beneficial, and he refuses to be mastered by anything. Christian liberty is genuine, but it is measured by love, by what builds up, and by the lordship of Christ, not by mere permission.

porneia.gloss: Sexual immorality of every kind, here especially union with a prostitute. Unlike sins committed outside the body, Paul says this sin is committed against one's own body, which belongs to the Lord and is joined to Christ, so the only faithful response is to flee.

naos.gloss: The inner sanctuary, the holy dwelling place of God, not merely the temple precincts. Paul calls the believer's very body a naos where the Holy Spirit lives. The presence of God once localized in Jerusalem now indwells the redeemed, making holiness of body a matter of worship.

agorazo.gloss: To buy, to purchase in the marketplace, the language of redemption. Believers were bought at a price, the blood of Christ, and so they no longer belong to themselves. Ownership has changed hands; the body is now the Lord's, to be used for His glory.

doxazo.gloss: To glorify, to honor, to make the worth of someone visible. The whole argument drives here: the redeemed body is not a private possession to indulge but an instrument to display the glory of God who made it, raised it, indwells it, and bought it.

basic.context: Some Corinthians were saying everything is permissible and using it to excuse visiting prostitutes, as if the body did not matter to God. Paul answers that the body matters enormously. It is made for the Lord, it will be raised like Christ's, it is joined to Christ, and it is the temple where the Holy Spirit lives. Because Jesus bought us with His own life, we are not our own, so we should run from sexual sin and use our bodies to honor God.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul takes the Corinthians' own slogans, everything is permissible and food for the stomach, and answers them point by point. He is not against freedom; he is against a freedom that ignores who Christ is, what the body is for, and who now owns us. The passage moves from freedom to lordship to redemption, and lands on a command to glorify God in the body.
basic.originalLanguages: The slogan is exestin, it is permitted, which Paul both grants and limits. The sin to flee is porneia, sexual immorality. The believer's body is a naos, the holy inner sanctuary where God dwells. And the reason we are not our own is agorazo, we were bought at a price, so the goal is to doxazo, to glorify God in the body.
basic.typology: The temple, once the house of God in Jerusalem, is fulfilled in Christ and now in His Spirit-indwelt people, whose bodies are living sanctuaries. The redemption language, bought at a price, fulfills the Passover and the ransom of Israel, now accomplished by the blood of Christ.
basic.christ: The body belongs to the Lord Jesus, who was raised bodily and will raise us. Our bodies are members of Christ, so joining them to sin dishonors Him personally. Above all, we were bought at a price: the price is Christ's own blood, and that purchase is why the body is His to glorify.
basic.god: God is not indifferent to the body; He made it, He will raise it, and He gives His Spirit to dwell in it. God is holy, and so His temple must be holy. God is also the great redeemer who paid the highest price to make rebels His own.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 12 calls us to offer our bodies as living sacrifices. 1 Corinthians 3 already called the church God's temple; here it narrows to the individual body. 1 Peter 1 says we were redeemed with the precious blood of Christ. Genesis 2 lies behind the one flesh union Paul cites.
basic.oneStory: The God who made human bodies in the garden, and whose glory once filled the temple, now dwells by His Spirit in the bodies of the redeemed. What sin defiles, Christ has bought back with His blood, so that the body becomes again what it was made to be, a place where God is glorified.

context.literary: The unit sits within Paul's response to reports of disorder in the church, following the lawsuits of 6:1-11 and leading into the marriage instructions of chapter 7. It is built around a chain of do you not know questions, quoted slogans that Paul answers, and a closing command. The argument rises from freedom to union with Christ to the indwelling Spirit and climaxes in the purchase price and the call to glorify God.
context.historical: Corinth was a port city notorious for sexual license, and temple prostitution and casual immorality were woven into its culture. Some converts imported a body-despising philosophy, treating physical appetites as spiritually neutral, like digesting food. Paul confronts this head on, insisting that the body has an eternal destiny and a present holiness.
context.canonical: This passage gathers the temple theology of the Old Testament, the one flesh union of Genesis, and the ransom theme of redemption, and focuses them on the Christian body. It stands alongside Romans 6 and 12 in teaching that the redeemed body is an instrument of righteousness and worship, not a throwaway shell.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To dismantle a false view of Christian freedom that excused sexual sin, and to establish a positive theology of the body: made for the Lord, joined to Christ, indwelt by the Spirit, bought by Christ, and therefore to be kept for God's glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The slogans Paul quotes are described in order to be corrected, not endorsed. The abiding prescriptions are clear: flee sexual immorality, and glorify God with your body. These are commands for every believer, grounded in unchanging truths about redemption and the indwelling Spirit.

debates.question: In what sense is sexual sin a sin against one's own body, unlike other sins?

views.label: A unique bodily and covenantal union
views.case: Sexual union creates a one flesh bond, so immorality violates the body in a way other sins do not, joining what belongs to Christ to sin itself.

views.label: A slogan Paul is qualifying
views.case: Some read the phrase as another Corinthian saying Paul answers; yet the flow best reads it as Paul's own point about the singular bodily nature of this sin.

debates.bearing: Either reading upholds Paul's command to flee; the mainstream reading stresses that immorality uniquely defiles the body that is joined to Christ and indwelt by the Spirit.

study.originalLanguages: Paul grants the slogan exestin, it is permitted, only to bind it under benefit and lordship: he will not be mastered by anything. He commands flight from porneia, sexual immorality, the one sin he says is committed against one's own body. The reason the body matters is that it is a naos, the holy inner sanctuary where the Spirit dwells, no longer a building in Jerusalem but the person of the believer. And the ground of the whole appeal is agorazo, we were bought at a price, a change of ownership sealed by Christ's blood, so that the fitting end of the body is to doxazo, to glorify God.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ and then in His people, whose Spirit-indwelt bodies are now the holy sanctuary where God is present and worshiped.
Fulfills the ransom of Israel and the Passover, now accomplished by the blood of Christ who purchases a people for God's own possession.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center of every line. The body is for the Lord, and the Lord for the body. God raised the Lord Jesus bodily, and will raise us with Him, which dignifies the body forever. Our bodies are members of Christ, so uniting them to immorality drags Christ's own members into sin. And the decisive fact is redemption: we were bought at a price, the price of Christ's blood, so the body now belongs to Him and exists to display His glory.
study.god: God is revealed as maker, raiser, indweller, and redeemer of the body. He is not a God who despises the physical; He made it, He will raise it imperishable, and He honors it by dwelling in it through His Spirit. He is holy, so His temple must be kept holy, and He is generous beyond measure, paying the highest price to claim His people as His own.

crossReferences.note: Offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God, the same logic of the body given wholly to God.
The church is God's temple; here Paul narrows the temple image to the individual believer's body.
You were redeemed with the precious blood of Christ, the price behind bought at a price.
The two will become one flesh, the union Paul cites to show the seriousness of sexual sin.
God's will is your sanctification, that you abstain from sexual immorality and control your body in holiness.

study.oneStory: From the garden God made human bodies good, and His glory later filled the temple in Jerusalem. Sin defiled both, but Christ bought back a people with His own blood, and now the Spirit makes the believer's body His temple. The command to flee immorality and glorify God in the body is the redeemed creature returning to its purpose: to house and honor the God who made it, raised it, and bought it.

quiz.q: How does Paul respond to the slogan everything is permissible?

options.text: He grants a measure of freedom but limits it: not everything is beneficial, and he will not be mastered.
options.note: Paul qualifies the slogan by benefit and by refusing to be enslaved to anything.

options.text: He fully endorses it as the heart of Christian liberty.
options.note: He immediately qualifies it; unlimited license is exactly what he corrects.

options.text: He says it applies only to food, never to the body.
options.note: He does distinguish food from the body, but his main point is that freedom is bounded by lordship.

options.text: He rejects all Christian freedom outright.
options.note: Paul affirms real freedom; he simply denies that it excuses sin.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say the body is not for sexual immorality?

options.text: Because the body is for the Lord, joined to Christ as His member.
options.note: The body belongs to the Lord and is a member of Christ, so it cannot be given to immorality.

options.text: Because the body is unimportant and will be discarded.
options.note: The opposite is true; God will raise the body, so it matters eternally.

options.text: Because sexual desire is inherently sinful.
options.note: Paul does not condemn the body or desire itself, but immorality that dishonors the Lord.

options.text: Because marriage is the only proper use of the body.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but Paul's stated reason is that the body is for the Lord.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by calling the body a temple of the Holy Spirit?

options.text: The Spirit dwells in the believer's body, making it a holy sanctuary for God.
options.note: The naos is God's dwelling; the Spirit indwells the believer, so the body is holy.

options.text: The body is a building we visit for worship.
options.note: The point is the reverse; the body itself is the dwelling, not a place we go.

options.text: Only the gathered church, not the individual, is God's temple.
options.note: The church is God's temple elsewhere, but here Paul speaks of the individual body.

options.text: The Spirit visits occasionally but does not dwell.
options.note: Paul says the Spirit is in you, a settled indwelling, not an occasional visit.

quiz.q: What is the ground of Paul's command to glorify God in the body?

options.text: You are not your own; you were bought at a price.
options.note: Redemption changes ownership: the body now belongs to the Lord who bought it.

options.text: You must earn your salvation by bodily discipline.
options.note: The logic is the reverse; because we are already bought, we glorify God.

options.text: Your body is your own to do with as you please.
options.note: Paul says precisely the opposite: you are not your own.

options.text: The body should be honored because it is naturally good.
options.note: The body is good, but Paul's stated ground is that we were bought at a price.

quiz.q: What price is behind the phrase bought at a price?

options.text: The blood of Christ, who redeemed us for God.
options.note: Redemption language points to the ransom paid in Christ's own blood.

options.text: Our own good works offered to God.
options.note: The price is Christ's, not ours; we are the ones purchased.

options.text: A general debt that remains unpaid.
options.note: The price has been fully paid, decisively, by Christ.

options.text: The cost of temple sacrifices we still must bring.
options.note: The old sacrifices pointed forward, but the price paid is Christ's blood, once for all.

questions: Everything is permissible, but not everything is beneficial. Where might you be using freedom in a way that is technically allowed but not truly good for you or others?
Paul says he will not be mastered by anything. What habit or appetite is quietly mastering you, and what would it look like to be free of it under Christ?
Your body is a member of Christ and a temple of the Holy Spirit. How does knowing that God dwells in you change the way you treat your body?
Paul's command is simply to flee sexual immorality. What practical steps of fleeing, not just resisting, do you need to take?
You are not your own; you were bought at a price. How would living as someone who belongs to Christ reshape your choices this week?

translationNotes.note: Everything is permissible for me appears in quotation marks because Paul is citing a Corinthian slogan and then qualifying it; the phrase is theirs, the correction is his.
Temple renders naos, the inner sanctuary or holy dwelling place, not the whole temple complex, stressing that the body is the very place where God's Spirit dwells.
Bought at a price uses the marketplace verb for purchase, the language of redemption; the price, unstated here, is the blood of Christ, and the result is that the body now belongs to God.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 6:12-20; any em dashes rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
